# pyScss, a Scss compiler for Python

{{ build-status }} {{ coverage }}

pyScss is a compiler for the [Sass] language, a superset of CSS3 that adds
programming capabilities and some other syntactic sugar.

## Quickstart

You need Python 2.6+ or 3.3+.  PyPy is also supported.

Installation:

```
pip install pyScss
```

Usage:

```
python -mscss < style.scss
```

Python API:

```
from scss import Compiler
Compiler().compile_string("a { color: red + green; }")
```

## Features

95% of Sass 3.2 is supported.  If it's not supported, it's a bug!  Please file
a ticket.

Most of Compass 0.11 is also built in.

## Further reading

Documentation is in Sphinx.  You can build it yourself by running `make html`
from within the `docs` directory, or read it on RTD:
<http://pyscss.readthedocs.org/en/latest/>

The canonical syntax reference is part of the Ruby Sass documentation:
<http://sass-lang.com/docs/yardoc/file.SASS_REFERENCE.html>

## Obligatory

documentation.

functions.name: scss.extension.core._interpret_percentage
documentation: Color creation

functions.name: scss.extension.core.mix
documentation:
Mixes together two colors. Specifically, takes the average of each of the
RGB components, optionally weighted by the given percentage.
The opacity of the colors is also considered when weighting the components.

Specifically, takes the average of each of the RGB components,
optionally weighted by the given percentage.
The opacity of the colors is also considered when weighting the components.

The weight specifies the amount of the first color that should be included
in the returned color.
50%, means that half the first color
    and half the second color should be used.
25% means that a quarter of the first color
    and three quarters of the second color should be used.

For example:

    mix(#f00, #00f) => #7f007f
    mix(#f00, #00f, 25%) => #3f00bf
    mix(rgba(255, 0, 0, 0.5), #00f) => rgba(63, 0, 191, 0.75)

classes.name: scss.extension.api.Extension

methods.name: scss.extension.api.Extension.handle_import
documentation:
Attempt to resolve an import.  Called once for every Sass string
listed in an `@import` statement.  Imports that Sass dictates should
be converted to plain CSS imports do NOT trigger this hook.

So this:

```
@import url(foo), "bar", "baz";
```

would call `handle_import` twice: once with "bar", once with "baz".

Return a {class}`scss.source.SourceFile` if you want to handle the
import, or None if you don't.  (This method returns None by default, so
if you don't care about hooking imports, just don't implement it.)
This method is tried on every registered `Extension` in order, until
one of them returns successfully.

A good example is the core Sass import machinery, which is implemented
with this hook; see the source code of the core extension.

class_variables.name: scss.extension.api.Extension.name
documentation:
A unique name for this extension, which will allow it to be referenced

class_variables.name: scss.extension.api.Extension.namespace

An optional {class}`scss.namespace.Namespace` that will be injected into

An extension to the Sass compile process.  Subclass to add your own
behavior.

Methods are hooks, called by the compiler at certain points.  Each
extension is considered in the order it's provided.

modules.name: scss.extension.compass.sprites
documentation:
Functions used for generating CSS sprites.

These are ported from the Compass sprite library:
http://compass-style.org/reference/compass/utilities/sprites/

functions.name: scss.extension.compass.sprites.alpha_composite
documentation: Compass-like functionality for sprites and images

functions.name: scss.extension.compass.sprites.sprite_map
documentation:
Generates a sprite map from the files matching the glob pattern.
Uses the keyword-style arguments passed in to control the placement.

$direction - Sprite map layout. Can be `vertical` (default), `horizontal`, `diagonal` or `smart`.

$position - For `horizontal` and `vertical` directions, the position of the sprite. (defaults to `0`)
$<sprite>-position - Position of a given sprite.

$padding, $spacing - Adds paddings to sprites (top, right, bottom, left). (defaults to `0, 0, 0, 0`)
$<sprite>-padding, $<sprite>-spacing - Padding for a given sprite.

$dst-color - Together with `$src-color`, forms a map of source colors to be converted to destiny colors (same index of `$src-color` changed to `$dst-color`).
$<sprite>-dst-color - Destiny colors for a given sprite. (defaults to `$dst-color`)

$src-color - Selects source colors to be converted to the corresponding destiny colors. (defaults to `black`)
$<sprite>-dst-color - Source colors for a given sprite. (defaults to `$src-color`)

$collapse - Collapses every image in the sprite map to a fixed size (`x` and `y`).
$collapse-x  - Collapses a size for `x`.
$collapse-y  - Collapses a size for `y`.

functions.name: scss.extension.compass.sprites.sprite_map_name
documentation:
Returns the name of a sprite map The name is derived from the folder than
contains the sprites.

functions.name: scss.extension.compass.sprites.sprite_file

Returns the relative path (from the images directory) to the original file
used when construction the sprite. This is suitable for passing to the

functions.name: scss.extension.compass.sprites.sprite_position

This is suitable for use as a value to background-position.

functions.name: scss.grammar.scanner.locate_blocks
documentation:
For processing CSS like strings.

Either returns all selectors (that can be "smart" multi-lined, as
long as it's joined by `,`, or enclosed in `(` and `)`) with its code block
(the one between `{` and `}`, which can be nested), or the "lose" code
(properties) that doesn't have any blocks.

modules.name: scss
documentation:
pyScss, a Scss compiler for Python

@author     German M. Bravo (Kronuz) <german.mb@gmail.com>
@version    1.2.0 alpha
@see        https://github.com/Kronuz/pyScss
@copyright  (c) 2012-2013 German M. Bravo (Kronuz)
@license    MIT License
            http://www.opensource.org/licenses/mit-license.php

pyScss compiles Scss, a superset of CSS that is more powerful, elegant and
easier to maintain than plain-vanilla CSS. The library acts as a CSS source code
preprocesor which allows you to use variables, nested rules, mixins, andhave
inheritance of rules, all with a CSS-compatible syntax which the preprocessor
then compiles to standard CSS.

Scss, as an extension of CSS, helps keep large stylesheets well-organized. It
borrows concepts and functionality from projects such as OOCSS and other similar
frameworks like as Sass. It's build on top of the original PHP xCSS codebase
structure but it's been completely rewritten, many bugs have been fixed and it
has been extensively extended to support almost the full range of Sass' Scss
syntax and functionality.

Bits of code in pyScss come from various projects:
Compass:
    (c) 2009 Christopher M. Eppstein
    http://compass-style.org/
Sass:
    (c) 2006-2009 Hampton Catlin and Nathan Weizenbaum
    http://sass-lang.com/
xCSS:
    (c) 2010 Anton Pawlik
    http://xcss.antpaw.org/docs/
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: scss.grammar.expression
documentation: Grammar for parsing Sass expressions.

modules.name: scss.ast
documentation:
Syntax tree for parsed Sass expressions.

The overall structure for a Sass file uses a different kind of AST; have a look
at {mod}`scss.blockast`.

methods.name: scss.ast.Expression.evaluate
documentation:
Evaluate this AST node, and return a Sass value.

`divide` indicates whether a descendant node representing a division
should be forcibly treated as a division.  See the commentary in
`BinaryOp`.

classes.name: scss.ast.Parentheses
documentation:
An expression of the form `(foo)`.

Only exists to force a slash to be interpreted as division when contained
within parentheses.

classes.name: scss.ast.Interpolation

methods.name: scss.ast.Interpolation.maybe
documentation:
Returns an interpolation if there are multiple parts, otherwise a
plain Literal.  This keeps the AST somewhat simpler, but also is the
only way `Literal.from_bareword` gets called.

A string that may contain any number of interpolations:

foo#{...}bar#{...}baz
TODO this class should delegate the unescaping to the type, rather than
burying it in the parser

classes.name: scss.ast.ArgspecLiteral

methods.name: scss.ast.ArgspecLiteral.iter_def_argspec
documentation:
Interpreting this literal as a function definition, yields pairs of
(variable name as a string, default value as an AST node or None).

methods.name: scss.ast.ArgspecLiteral.evaluate_call_args
documentation:
Interpreting this literal as a function call, return a 2-tuple of
`(args, kwargs)`.

Contains pairs of argument names and values, as parsed from a function
definition or function call.

Note that the semantics are somewhat ambiguous.  Consider parsing:

    $foo, $bar: 3

If this appeared in a function call, $foo would refer to a value; if it
appeared in a function definition, $foo would refer to an existing
variable.  This it's up to the caller to use the right iteration function.

classes.name: scss.ast.FunctionLiteral
documentation: Wraps an existing AST node in a literal (unevaluated) function call.

classes.name: scss.ast.AlphaFunctionLiteral
documentation:
Wraps an existing AST node in a literal (unevaluated) function call,
prepending "opacity=" to the contents.

classes.name: scss.ast.TernaryOp
documentation:
Sass implements this with a function:

    prop: if(condition, true-value, false-value);

However, the second and third arguments are guaranteed not to be evaluated
unless necessary.  Functions always receive evaluated arguments, so this is
a syntactic construct in disguise.

modules.name: scss.tool
documentation: !/usr/bin/env python

functions.name: scss.compiler.compile_file
documentation:
Compile a single file (provided as a {class}`pathlib.Path`), and return
a string of CSS.

Keyword arguments are passed along to the underlying `Compiler`.

Note that the search path is set to the file's containing directory by
default, unless you explicitly pass a `search_path` kwarg.

```
param filename

Path to the file to compile.

type filename

str, bytes, or 
```

functions.name: scss.compiler.compile_string
documentation:
Compile a single string, and return a string of CSS.

Keyword arguments are passed along to the underlying `Compiler`.

classes.name: scss.compiler.Compiler

methods.name: scss.compiler.Compiler.__init__
documentation:
Configure a compiler.

```
param root

Directory to treat as the "project root".  Search paths
and some custom extensions (e.g. Compass) are relative to this
directory.  Defaults to the current directory.

type root



param search_path

List of paths to search for @import``s, relative
to ``root.  Absolute and parent paths are allowed here, but
@import will refuse to load files that aren't in one of the
directories here.  Defaults to only the root.

type search_path

list of strings,  objects, or
something that implements a similar interface (useful for custom
pseudo filesystems)
```

methods.name: scss.compiler.Compiler.call_and_catch_errors
documentation:
Call the given function with the given arguments.  If it succeeds,
return its return value.  If it raises a {class}`scss.errors.SassError`
and `live_errors` is turned on, return CSS containing a traceback and
error message.

A Sass compiler.  Stores settings and knows how to fire off a
compilation.  Main entry point into compiling Sass.

methods.name: scss.compiler.Compilation.parse_selectors

methods.name: scss.compiler.Compilation.apply_extends

declarations into real selectors on parent rules.

The list is modified in-place and also sorted in dependency order.

A single run of a compiler.

classes.name: scss.compiler.SassReturn
documentation:
Special control-flow exception used to hop up the stack from a Sass
function's `@return`.

functions.name: scss.selector._is_combinator_subset_of
documentation:
Return whether `specific` matches a non-strict subset of what `general`
matches.

functions.name: scss.selector._merge_selectors
documentation:
Given two selector chains (lists of simple selectors), return a list of
selector chains representing elements matched by both of them.

This operation is not exact, and involves some degree of fudging -- the
wackier and more divergent the input, the more fudging.  It's meant to be
what a human might expect rather than a precise covering of all possible
cases.  Most notably, when the two input chains have absolutely nothing in
common, the output is merely `left + right` and `right + left` rather
than all possible interleavings.

functions.name: scss.selector._weave_conflicting_selectors
documentation:
Part of the selector merge algorithm above.  Not useful on its own.  Pay
no attention to the man behind the curtain.

functions.name: scss.selector._merge_simple_selectors
documentation:
Merge two simple selectors, for the purposes of the LCS algorithm below.

In practice this returns the more specific selector if one is a subset of
the other, else it returns None.

functions.name: scss.selector.longest_common_subsequence
documentation:
Find the longest common subsequence between two iterables.

The longest common subsequence is the core of any diff algorithm: it's the
longest sequence of elements that appears in both parent sequences in the
same order, but NOT necessarily consecutively.

Original algorithm borrowed from Wikipedia:
<http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Longest_common_subsequence_problem#Code_for_the_dynamic_programming_solution>

This function is used only to implement @extend, largely because that's
what the Ruby implementation does.  Thus it's been extended slightly from
the simple diff-friendly algorithm given above.

What @extend wants to know is whether two simple selectors are compatible,
not just equal.  To that end, you must pass in a "merge" function to
compare a pair of elements manually.  It should return `None` if they are
incompatible, and a MERGED element if they are compatible -- in the case of
selectors, this is whichever one is more specific.

Because of this fuzzier notion of equality, the return value is a list of
`(a_index, b_index, value)` tuples rather than items alone.

classes.name: scss.selector.SimpleSelector

methods.name: scss.selector.SimpleSelector.is_superset_of
documentation:
Return True iff this selector matches the same elements as `other`,
and perhaps others.

That is, `.foo` is a superset of `.foo.bar`, because the latter is
more specific.

Set `soft_combinator` true to ignore the specific case of this selector
having a descendent combinator and `other` having anything else.  This
is for superset checking for `@extend`, where a space combinator
really means "none".

methods.name: scss.selector.SimpleSelector.replace_parent
documentation:
If `&` (or the legacy xCSS equivalent `self`) appears in this
selector, replace it with the given iterable of parent selectors.

Returns a tuple of simple selectors.

methods.name: scss.selector.SimpleSelector.merge_into
documentation:
Merge two simple selectors together.  This is expected to be the
selector being injected into `other` -- that is, `other` is the
selector for a block using `@extend`, and `self` is a selector being
extended.

Element tokens must come first, and pseudo-element tokens must come
last, and there can only be one of each.  The final selector thus looks
something like:

```
[element] [misc self tokens] [misc other tokens] [pseudo-element]
```

This method does not check for duplicate tokens; those are assumed to
have been removed earlier, during the search for a hinge.

A simple selector, by CSS 2.1 terminology: a combination of element
name, class selectors, id selectors, and other criteria that all apply to a
single element.

Note that CSS 3 considers EACH of those parts to be a "simple selector",
and calls a group of them a "sequence of simple selectors".  That's a
terrible class name, so we're going with 2.1 here.

For lack of a better name, each of the individual parts is merely called a
"token".

Note that it's possible to have zero tokens.  This isn't legal CSS, but
it's perfectly legal Sass, since you might nest blocks like so:

    body > {
        div {
            ...
        }
    }

classes.name: scss.selector.Selector

methods.name: scss.selector.Selector.__init__
documentation:
Return a selector containing a sequence of `SimpleSelector`s.

You probably want to use `parse_many` or `parse_one` instead.

methods.name: scss.selector.Selector.lookup_key
documentation:
Build a key from the "important" parts of a selector: elements,
classes, ids.

methods.name: scss.selector.Selector.substitute
documentation:
Return a list of selectors obtained by replacing the `target`
selector with `replacement`.

Herein lie the guts of the Sass @extend directive.

In general, for a selector `a X b Y c`, a target `X Y`, and a
replacement `q Z`, return the selectors `a q X b Z c` and `q a X b
Z c`.  Note in particular that no more than two selectors will be
returned, and the permutation of ancestors will never insert new simple
selectors "inside" the target selector.

methods.name: scss.selector.Selector.break_around
documentation:
Given a simple selector node contained within this one (a "hinge"),
break it in half and return a parent selector, extra specifiers for the
hinge, and a child selector.

That is, given a hinge X, break the selector A + X.y B into A, + .y,
and B.
A single CSS selector.

modules.name: scss.cssdefs
documentation:
Constants and functions defined by the CSS specification, not specific to
Sass.

functions.name: scss.cssdefs.get_conversion_factor
documentation:
Look up the "base" unit for this unit and the factor for converting to
it.

Returns a 2-tuple of `factor, base_unit`.

functions.name: scss.cssdefs.convert_units_to_base_units
documentation:
Convert a set of units into a set of "base" units.

Returns a 2-tuple of `factor, new_units`.

functions.name: scss.cssdefs.count_base_units
documentation:
Returns a dict mapping names of base units to how many times they
appear in the given iterable of units.  Effectively this counts how
many length units you have, how many time units, and so forth.

functions.name: scss.cssdefs.cancel_base_units
documentation:
Given a list of units, remove a specified number of each base unit.

Arguments:
    units: an iterable of units
    to_remove: a mapping of base_unit => count, such as that returned from
        count_base_units

Returns a 2-tuple of (factor, remaining_units).

functions.name: scss.cssdefs.is_builtin_css_function
documentation:
Returns whether the given `name` looks like the name of a builtin CSS
function.

Unrecognized functions not in this list produce warnings.

functions.name: scss.cssdefs.determine_encoding
documentation:
Return the appropriate encoding for the given CSS source, according to
the CSS charset rules.

`buf` may be either a string or bytes.
CSS character set determination
Based upon: http://www.w3.org/TR/CSS2/syndata.html#charset

functions.name: scss.cssdefs.unescape
documentation:
Given a raw CSS string (i.e. taken directly from CSS source with no
processing), eliminate all backslash escapes.

modules.name: scss.namespace
documentation: Support for Sass's namespacing rules.

classes.name: scss.namespace.Scope
documentation:
Implements Sass variable scoping.

Similar to `ChainMap`, except that assigning a new value will replace an
existing value, not mask it.

methods.name: scss.namespace.Namespace.derive
documentation:
Return a new child namespace.  All existing variables are still
readable and writeable, but any new variables will only exist within a
new scope.

methods.name: scss.namespace.Namespace.declare
documentation:
Insert a Python function into this Namespace, detecting its name and
argument count automatically.

methods.name: scss.namespace.Namespace.declare_alias
documentation:
Insert a Python function into this Namespace with an
explicitly-given name, but detect its argument count automatically.

methods.name: scss.namespace.Namespace.declare_internal
documentation:
Like declare(), but the registered function will also receive the
current namespace as its first argument.  Useful for functions that
inspect the state of the compilation, like `variable-exists()`.
Probably not so useful for anything else.

classes.name: scss.source.SourceFile

methods.name: scss.source.SourceFile.__init__
documentation:
Not normally used.  See the three alternative constructors:
{func}`SourceFile.from_file`, {func}`SourceFile.from_path`, and
{func}`SourceFile.from_string`.

methods.name: scss.source.SourceFile.path
documentation:
Concatenation of `origin` and `relpath`, as a string.  Used in
stack traces and other debugging places.

methods.name: scss.source.SourceFile.read
documentation:
Read a source file from an `(origin, relpath)` tuple, as would
happen from an `@import` statement.

methods.name: scss.source.SourceFile.from_path
documentation:
Read Sass source from a {class}`pathlib.Path`.

If no origin is given, it's assumed to be the file's parent directory.

methods.name: scss.source.SourceFile.from_filename
documentation: Read Sass source from a String specifying the path
back-compat

methods.name: scss.source.SourceFile.from_file
documentation:
Read Sass source from a file or file-like object.

If `origin` or `relpath` are missing, they are derived from the file's
`.name` attribute as with `from_path`.  If it doesn't have one, the
origin becomes None and the relpath becomes the file's repr.

methods.name: scss.source.SourceFile.from_string
documentation:
Read Sass source from the contents of a string.

The origin is always None.  `relpath` defaults to "string:...".

class_variables.name: scss.source.SourceFile.key
documentation:
A 2-tuple of `(origin, relpath)` that uniquely identifies where the
file came from and how to find its siblings.

A single input file to be fed to the compiler.  Detects the encoding
(according to CSS spec rules) and performs some light pre-processing.

This class is mostly internal and you shouldn't have to worry about it.

Source files are uniquely identified by their `.key`, a 2-tuple of
`(origin, relpath)`.

`origin` is an object from the compiler's search
path, most often a directory represented by a {class}`pathlib.Path`.
`relpath` is a relative path from there to the actual file, again usually
a `Path`.

The idea here is that source files don't always actually come from the
filesystem, yet import semantics are expressed in terms of paths.  By
keeping the origin and relative path separate, it's possible for e.g.
Django to swap in an object that has the `Path` interface, but actually
looks for files in an arbitrary storage backend.  In that case it would
make no sense to key files by their absolute path, as they may not exist on
disk or even on the same machine.  Also, relative imports can then continue
to work, because they're guaranteed to only try the same origin.

The `origin` may thus be anything that implements a minimal ``` Path``ish
interface (division operator, ``.parent ```, `.resolve()`).  It may also be
`None`, indicating that the file came from a string or some other origin
that can't usefully produce other files.

`relpath`, however, should always be a `Path`. or string.  XXX only when origin  (There's little
advantage to making it anything else.)  A `relpath` may **never** contain
".."; there is nothing above the origin.

Note that one minor caveat of this setup is that it's possible for the same
file on disk to be imported under two different names (even though symlinks
are always resolved), if directories in the search path happen to overlap.
TODO i'm still not entirely happy with this, nor with the concept of an
"origin".  it should really be a "loader", with a defined API.  also, even
with all these helpful classmethods, i'm still having to do a lot of manual
mucking around in django-pyscss, where all i'm given is a file path and a
string of the contents, and i want to /not/ re-read the file.

classes.name: scss.legacy.Scss

methods.name: scss.legacy.Scss.compile
documentation:
Compile Sass to CSS.  Returns a single CSS string.

This method is DEPRECATED; see {mod}`scss.compiler` instead.

Original programmatic interface to the compiler.

This class is now DEPRECATED.  See {mod}`scss.compiler` for the
replacement.

functions.name: scss.types._constrain
documentation: Helper for Color constructors.  Constrains a value to a range.

classes.name: scss.types.Value

methods.name: scss.types.Value.__bool__
documentation: Sass values are all true, except for booleans and nulls

methods.name: scss.types.Value.__eq__
documentation: ## NOTE: From here on down, the operators are exposed to Sass code and
## thus should ONLY return Sass types
Reasonable default for equality

methods.name: scss.types.Value.__lt__
documentation: Only numbers support ordering

methods.name: scss.types.Value.__add__
documentation: Math ops

methods.name: scss.types.Value.__truediv__
documentation: Sass types have no notion of floor vs true division

methods.name: scss.types.Value.to_dict
documentation:
Return the Python dict equivalent of this map.

If this type can't be expressed as a map, raise.

methods.name: scss.types.Value.to_pairs
documentation:
Return the Python list-of-tuples equivalent of this map.  Note that
this is different from `self.to_dict().items()`, because Sass maps
preserve order.

If this type can't be expressed as a map, raise.

methods.name: scss.types.Value.render
documentation:
Return this value's CSS representation as a string (text, i.e.
unicode!).

If `compress` is true, try hard to shorten the string at the cost of
readability.

methods.name: scss.types.Value.render_interpolated
documentation:
Return this value's string representation as appropriate for
returning from an interpolation.
pyScss data types:
TODO make Value work as a string in every way?  i.e. have a .quotes...

methods.name: scss.types.Number._add_sub
documentation: Implements both addition and subtraction.

methods.name: scss.types.Number.to_base_units
documentation:
Convert to a fixed set of "base" units.  The particular units are
arbitrary; what's important is that they're consistent.

Used for addition and comparisons.
## Helper methods, mostly used internally

methods.name: scss.types.Number.wrap_python_function
documentation:
Wraps an unary Python math function, translating the argument from
Sass to Python on the way in, and vice versa for the return value.

Used to wrap simple Python functions like `ceil`, `floor`, etc.
## Utilities for public consumption

methods.name: scss.types.Number.to_python_index
documentation:
Return a plain Python integer appropriate for indexing a sequence of
the given length.  Raise if this is impossible for any reason
whatsoever.

methods.name: scss.types.Number.has_simple_unit
documentation:
Returns True iff the unit is expressible in CSS, i.e., has no
denominator and at most one unit in the numerator.

methods.name: scss.types.Number.is_simple_unit
documentation:
Return True iff the unit is simple (as above) and matches the given
unit.

classes.name: scss.types.List

methods.name: scss.types.List.maybe_new
documentation:
If `values` contains only one item, return that item.  Otherwise,
return a List as normal.

methods.name: scss.types.List.maybe
documentation:
If this List contains only one item, return it.  Otherwise, return
the List.

methods.name: scss.types.List.from_maybe
documentation:
If `values` appears to not be a list, return a list containing it.
Otherwise, return a List as normal.

methods.name: scss.types.List.from_maybe_starargs
documentation:
If `args` has one element which appears to be a list, return it.
Otherwise, return a list as normal.

Mainly used by Sass function implementations that predate `...`
support, so they can accept both a list of arguments and a single list
stored in a variable.

methods.name: scss.types.List.__add__
documentation: DEVIATION: binary ops on lists and scalars act element-wise

A list of other values.  May be delimited by commas or spaces.

Lists of one item don't make much sense in CSS, but can exist in Sass.  Use ......

Lists may also contain zero items, but these are forbidden from appearing
in CSS output.

classes.name: scss.types.Arglist
documentation:
An argument list.  Acts mostly like a list, with keyword arguments sort
of tacked on separately, and only accessible via Python (or the Sass
`keywords` function).

methods.name: scss.types.Color.from_rgb
documentation: ## Alternate constructors

methods.name: scss.types.Color.from_name
documentation: Build a Color from a CSS color name.

methods.name: scss.types.Color.rgb
documentation: ## Accessors

methods.name: scss.types.Color.render
documentation:
Return a rendered representation of the color.  If `compress` is
true, the shortest possible representation is used; otherwise, named
colors are rendered as names and all others are rendered as hex (or
with the rgba function).

classes.name: scss.types.String

methods.name: scss.types.String.unquoted
documentation: Helper to create a string with no quotes.

methods.name: scss.types.String._escape_character
documentation: Given a single character, return it appropriately CSS-escaped.

Represents both CSS quoted string values and CSS identifiers (such as
`left`).

Makes no distinction between single and double quotes, except that the same
quotes are preserved on string literals that pass through unmodified.
Otherwise, double quotes are used.

classes.name: scss.types.Function
documentation:
Function call pseudo-type, which crops up frequently in CSS as a string
marker.  Acts mostly like a string, but has a function name and parentheses
around it.
TODO this needs to pretend the url(...) is part of the string for all string
operations -- even the quotes!  alas.
TODO recasting a function to a String will lose the function part?  whoops.
maybe .value should just be, uh, the literal value instead of the insides???

modules.name: scss.scss_meta
documentation:
pyScss, a Scss compiler for Python

@author     German M. Bravo (Kronuz) <german.mb@gmail.com>
@version    1.4.0
@see        https://github.com/Kronuz/pyScss
@copyright  (c) 2012-2013 German M. Bravo (Kronuz)
@license    MIT License
            http://www.opensource.org/licenses/mit-license.php

pyScss compiles Scss, a superset of CSS that is more powerful, elegant and
easier to maintain than plain-vanilla CSS. The library acts as a CSS source code
preprocesor which allows you to use variables, nested rules, mixins, andhave
inheritance of rules, all with a CSS-compatible syntax which the preprocessor
then compiles to standard CSS.

Scss, as an extension of CSS, helps keep large stylesheets well-organized. It
borrows concepts and functionality from projects such as OOCSS and other similar
frameworks like as Sass. It's build on top of the original PHP xCSS codebase
structure but it's been completely rewritten, many bugs have been fixed and it
has been extensively extended to support almost the full range of Sass' Scss
syntax and functionality.

Bits of code in pyScss come from various projects:
Compass:
    (c) 2009 Christopher M. Eppstein
    http://compass-style.org/
Sass:
    (c) 2006-2009 Hampton Catlin and Nathan Weizenbaum
    http://sass-lang.com/
xCSS:
    (c) 2010 Anton Pawlik
    http://xcss.antpaw.org/docs/

    This file defines Meta data, according to PEP314
    (http://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0314/) which is common to both pyScss
    and setup.py distutils.

    We create this here so this information can be compatible with BOTH
    Python 2.x and Python 3.x so setup.py can use it when building pyScss
    for both Py3.x and Py2.x
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

variables.name: scss.scss_meta.DATE_INFO
documentation: YEAR, MONTH, DAY

variables.name: scss.config.FONTS_ROOT
documentation: default: STATIC_ROOT

variables.name: scss.config.IMAGES_ROOT
documentation: default: STATIC_ROOT

variables.name: scss.config.FONTS_URL
documentation: default: STATIC_URL

variables.name: scss.config.IMAGES_URL
documentation: default: STATIC_URL

functions.name: scss.rule.extend_unique
documentation:
Return a new sequence containing the items in `seq` plus any items in
`more` that aren't already in `seq`, preserving the order of both.

classes.name: scss.rule.SassRule

methods.name: scss.rule.SassRule.file_and_line
documentation:
Return the filename and line number where this rule originally
appears, in the form "foo.scss:3".  Used for error messages.

methods.name: scss.rule.SassRule.is_empty
documentation:
Return whether this rule is considered "empty" -- i.e., has no
contents that should end up in the final CSS.

At its heart, a CSS rule: combination of a selector and zero or more
properties.  But this is Sass, so it also tracks some Sass-flavored
metadata, like `@extend` rules and `@media` nesting.

methods.name: scss.rule.RuleAncestry.with_more_selectors
documentation:
Return a new ancestry that also matches the given selectors.  No
nesting is done.

classes.name: scss.rule.UnparsedBlock

methods.name: scss.rule.UnparsedBlock.is_atrule
documentation: ## What kind of thing is this?
